data.title: Capricor Therapeutics (CAPR) Posts US$50 Million H1 Loss Reinforcing Bearish Cash Burn Narrative
data.description:
Capricor Therapeutics (CAPR) has just posted its FY 2025 first half results with total revenue of US$0 million and a basic EPS loss of US$1.10, while trailing twelve month revenue sits at US$13.39 million with a basic EPS loss of US$1.66. Over recent periods, the company has reported revenue of US$8.88 million and US$13.39 million across the two halves of FY 2024, alongside basic EPS losses of US$0.66 and US$0.51. This provides context for how current margins remain under pressure. With revenue still modest and losses ongoing, the focus for investors is on how quickly margins can improve from here.

See our full analysis for Capricor Therapeutics.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile compares with the widely shared narratives around Capricor’s growth potential, risk profile, and path toward better margins.

See what the community is saying about Capricor Therapeutics

Advertisement

US$50 million first half loss as costs climb

  • In the first half of FY 2025, Capricor reported a net loss of US$50.3 million on zero revenue, compared with losses of US$20.8 million and US$19.7 million on US$8.9 million and US$13.4 million of revenue in the two halves of FY 2024.
  • Bears point to this widening loss profile as a sign that cash burn is getting harder to ignore, especially with trailing twelve month net loss at US$70.0 million while product revenue is not yet contributing.
    • The bearish narrative highlights rising research and development and operating expenses, and the H1 2025 numbers align with that view by showing a much larger loss than either half of FY 2024 on a smaller revenue base.
    • Critics also stress Capricor’s reliance on a single main program, and the move from US$22.3 million of trailing revenue a year earlier to US$13.4 million on the latest trailing view leaves less offset to those operating costs.
Stay grounded when you look at headline losses like this, and see how skeptics frame the risks around Deramiocel and cash burn in the detailed bear case for Capricor: 🐻 Capricor Therapeutics Bear Case

Losses widening even as forecasts point to strong growth

  • Over the last twelve months, Capricor generated US$13.4 million in revenue and a net loss of US$70.0 million, while analysts are forecasting revenue growth of 37.7% a year and earnings growth of about 65.8% a year with an expected move into profitability within three years.
  • Supporters argue this forecast profile heavily backs a bullish view that today’s losses are the price of building future earnings power, but the gap between the US$70.0 million trailing loss and the positive earnings forecast keeps execution risk front and center.
    • The bullish narrative leans on expectations that earnings could swing from a loss of roughly US$70.0 million today to positive earnings in the US$5.6 million to US$14.4 million range by 2028, which would be a very large shift from the current run rate.
    • At the same time, five year loss growth of about 30.6% per year shows that, so far, higher spending has not yet translated into improving profitability in the reported numbers, so those growth forecasts are not reflected in the recent income statement yet.
If you are weighing big growth forecasts against current losses, it helps to read how bullish investors connect these numbers to their thesis on Capricor: 🐂 Capricor Therapeutics Bull Case

Rich valuation multiples versus peers despite DCF gap

  • The shares trade at a P/B of 20.9x compared with 2.6x for the US Biotechs industry, while a DCF fair value of US$312.47 per share sits far above the current price of US$30.50.
  • Consensus narrative commentary often points to substantial upside relative to valuation models and price targets, yet the combination of a very high P/B multiple and ongoing losses means the market is already paying a premium to book value while waiting for the projected turn to profitability.
    • On one side, the stock is described as trading about 90.2% below the cited DCF fair value of US$312.47, which is a very large implied upside if those cash flow assumptions are met.
    • On the other, trailing losses of US$70.0 million and a P/B of 20.9x compared with 2.6x for peers indicate investors today are funding a loss making business at a multiple far above the sector, which lines up with bears’ concern that valuation is demanding relative to current fundamentals.

data.description:

  • The share price has seen a 71.0% return over the past year, even though the stock is down 10.0% over the last week, 12.9% over the last month, and 8.0% year to date. This can change how investors think about both its growth potential and risk.

Approach 1: Establishment Labs Holdings Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes projected future cash flows and discounts them back to today using a required rate of return, to estimate what the business might be worth per share right now.

For Establishment Labs Holdings, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of $69.12 million. Analysts have provided free cash flow estimates out to 2028, with projections extended further using Simply Wall St assumptions. The ten year free cash flow path runs from $16 million in 2026 up to about $172 million in 2035, all in dollar terms.

Discounting those projected cash flows back to today produces an estimated intrinsic value of about $81.38 per share. Compared with the recent share price around $63.35, the DCF output suggests the stock is 22.2% undervalued on this model.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Establishment Labs Holdings.

Approach 2: Establishment Labs Holdings Price vs Sales

For companies where earnings are not yet positive or are volatile, the P/S ratio is often a useful way to compare what investors are paying for each dollar of revenue. It avoids some of the noise that can come from temporary swings in profit, while still reflecting how the market views the business.

Expectations for growth and the level of risk usually influence what looks like a normal or fair P/S multiple. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify paying a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher risk can point to a lower one.

Establishment Labs currently trades on a P/S of 8.80x. That sits well above the Medical Equipment industry average of 2.76x and also above its peer group average of 1.59x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for the company is 4.25x. This Fair Ratio is a proprietary estimate of the P/S multiple that might be appropriate given factors such as earnings growth, industry, profit margins, market cap and risk.

Because it blends these company specific drivers, the Fair Ratio is designed to be more tailored than a simple comparison with peers or the broad industry. Setting 8.80x against the Fair Ratio of 4.25x points to Establishment Labs trading at a richer valuation on this measure.

Result: OVERVALUED

Guidance points to profit to loss swing

Star Plus Legend Holdings (SEHK:6683) has guided for a consolidated loss of up to CN¥52.0 million for 2025, compared with a consolidated profit of CN¥50.2 million for 2024.

The board links this shift mainly to slower IP content launches affecting its IP creation segment, lower gross margins in the expanding new retail business, and a non cash impairment provision on certain trade receivables tied to recent geopolitical developments.

See our latest analysis for Star Plus Legend Holdings.

Despite the profit to loss guidance, Star Plus Legend Holdings’ 1 day share price return of 16.86% pushed the stock to HK$8.25. Its 7 day share price return of 31.58% and 1 year total shareholder return of 96.90% suggest building momentum around shifting expectations for its IP and retail businesses.

If this update has you thinking about where else growth stories might emerge, it could be a good time to scan our screener of 98 top founder-led companies for fresh ideas beyond Star Plus Legend Holdings.

With a forecast swing from CN¥50.2 million profit to up to CN¥52.0 million loss and a share price that has already doubled over 1 year, is this a mispriced setback or a market that is already banking on future growth?

Preferred price to sales of 10.3x, is it justified?

On a P/S of 10.3x at a last close of HK$8.25, Star Plus Legend Holdings trades at a much richer revenue multiple than many peers in its sector.

The P/S ratio compares the company’s market value with its revenue. A 10.3x reading means investors are currently paying HK$10.30 in market value for every HK$1 of sales. For a business focused on health products, celebrity IP and retail, this kind of premium often reflects expectations around future scaling of revenue or earnings quality that goes beyond the latest reported figures.

That premium looks particularly stretched when you compare it with the Hong Kong Food industry average P/S of 0.8x and a peer average of 1.1x, both far below 10.3x. With insufficient data to cross check this against a fair P/S ratio or detailed cash flow forecasts, you are mostly relying on your own conviction about the durability of its IP model, the new retail expansion and the guidance for a swing into loss in 2025.

data.title: Butler National (OTCPK:BUKS) Margin Expansion Reinforces Bullish Earnings Narratives In Q3 2026
data.description:
Butler National (BUKS) has reported Q3 2026 revenue of US$26.9 million and basic EPS of US$0.10, set against trailing twelve month revenue of US$91.9 million and EPS of US$0.30 that came with 44.1% earnings growth and a 21.4% net margin over the past year. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from US$21.2 million in Q3 2025 to US$26.9 million in Q3 2026 and basic EPS from US$0.05 to US$0.10 across the same periods, with trailing net income reaching US$19.7 million, which positions the latest release as part of a period where profitability and margins are central to how investors read the story.

See our full analysis for Butler National.

With the numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up against the prevailing narratives around Butler National, including where the data backs those stories and where it pushes back.

See what the community is saying about Butler National

Advertisement

44.1% TTM earnings growth behind the headline

  • Over the last 12 months, Butler National generated US$19.7 million of net income and Basic EPS of US$0.30 on US$91.9 million of revenue, with earnings reported as growing 44.1% year over year.
  • What is striking for a bullish view is how the 44.1% earnings growth lines up with a 29.9% five year annual earnings growth rate. This suggests recent performance is stronger than the longer term trend:
    • Supporters pointing to consistency over time can reference that the company has been profitable for at least five years, with that 29.9% annual growth rate backing a longer track record rather than a one off year.
    • At the same time, anyone leaning on a bullish angle still needs to keep in mind that this acceleration is judged only on trailing figures. The key question is whether the current US$6.7 million Q3 net income fits into a steady pattern or a particularly strong run.

Margins sit at 21.4% on a trailing basis

  • Trailing net margin is reported at 21.4%, compared with 16.1% the prior year, while Q3 2026 net income of US$6.7 million on US$26.9 million of revenue gives a similar picture of profitability on recent sales.
  • Bears who question the durability of higher profitability have to weigh that concern against the margin profile now on record:
    • The step from a 16.1% net margin last year to 21.4% on a trailing basis sits alongside quarterly net income rising from US$3.3 million in Q4 2025 to US$6.7 million in Q3 2026. Recent quarters therefore echo the stronger profitability seen in the trailing figures.
    • On the other hand, skeptics can fairly point out that margins are being viewed only over a one year comparison window, so they may want to see how future quarters compare to the current 21.4% level before calling it a settled trend.

P/E of 12.1x against richer industry multiples

  • At a share price of US$3.70 and trailing Basic EPS of US$0.30, Butler National trades on a P/E of 12.1x, which sits well below both the reported peer average of 35.7x and the US Aerospace & Defense industry average of 42.3x. The DCF fair value is cited at US$5.66 per share versus the current market price.
  • What stands out for investors weighing a balanced narrative is the gap between these valuation measures and the earnings profile:
    • On one side, the DCF fair value of US$5.66 compared to a US$3.70 share price, along with a 21.4% net margin and 44.1% trailing earnings growth, gives quantitative support for investors who view the current multiple as conservative relative to both fundamentals and the higher peer and industry P/E levels.
    • On the other side, the fact that the market is still assigning a 12.1x P/E despite those metrics leaves room for the view that investors may be pricing in company specific risks or simply applying a discount compared with the wider Aerospace & Defense group.

Curious how other investors are interpreting these numbers compared to the valuation and growth history? 📊 Read the what the Community is saying about Butler National.
